content.text: MCP tunnel architecture (no inbound ports)
is an
outbound-only connectivity pattern
that lets a cloud-hosted Claude agent talk to an MCP server inside your private network (VPC, on-premises, or local dev machine)
without opening any inbound firewall ports
. Instead of the MCP server exposing a public endpoint and waiting for inbound connections, you deploy a
lightweight gateway/client
inside your network that initiates a single
persistent outbound HTTPS connection

to the AI provider's tunnel infrastructure. When the model wants to call a tool, the request flows through that established connection in reverse, so the firewall sees only normal outbound traffic—identical to a web browser—never an inbound request.

attrs.alt: MCP tunnel architecture (no inbound ports) concept diagram

content.text: What it is

The architecture reverses the connection direction:
1.
Tunnel client/gateway

: Small binary部署ed alongside the private MCP server inside your network.
2.
Outbound connection

: Client establishes a persistent, encrypted HTTPS connection to the AI provider's tunnel edge (e.g., Cloudflare-managed TLS).
3.
Long-poll mechanism

: Client waits (long-polls) for queued work from the provider.
4.
Reverse request flow

: When the model calls a tool, the request is queued in the tunnel; client picks it up, forwards the JSON-RPC request to the local MCP server over stdio or HTTP, and posts the response back through the same channel.

Key properties:
-
No inbound ports

: The MCP server never initiates a connection to anything and never touches the public internet.
-
No public endpoint

: No public DNS entry, no public listener on the MCP server.
-
No VPN required

: Just a single outbound HTTPS connection.
-
End-to-end encryption
: Tunnel uses TLS to the edge and re-encrypted transport to the AI provider.
Why it matters

Without MCP tunnels:
-
Security review fails

: Exposing internal tools traditionally means opening inbound firewall ports or putting internal tools behind a public endpoint. This fails security review almost every time.
-
Network exposure

: Inbound ports create a risk surface (attackers can probe the public endpoint).
-
Brittle IP allowlists

: You maintain IP whitelists that break when the model's IP changes.
-
Manual DNS setup
: You need public DNS entries for the MCP server.

With MCP tunnels:
-
Security-friendly

: From the firewall's perspective, this is normal outbound HTTPS traffic—already allowed by most corporate networks, no negotiation required.
-
Zero inbound exposure

: No inbound ports open, no public endpoint, no network exposure.
-
Simplified deployment

: One secret (the gateway's tunnel credential), no public DNS, no VPN.
-
Enterprise-ready
: Works with ChatGPT connectors, Codex sessions, and Responses API calls simultaneously.
This is critical for
enterprise deployments
where security teams block MCP deployments that require inbound ports.
Mental model
Think of MCP tunnels as
reversing a door

:
- Traditional setup: The MCP server opens a door to the outside and waits for visitors (inbound connections).
- MCP tunnel: The tunnel client opens a door to the outside and waits for the provider to send visitors (outbound connection).
- The firewall sees only the client walking out the door, never someone walking in.
Or think of it as
Cloudflare Tunnel/ngrok

:
- Cloudflare Tunnel: A small client runs inside your network, opens an outbound HTTPS connection to Cloudflare, and waits for inbound requests routed over that connection.
- MCP Tunnel: Same pattern, but for MCP servers connecting to AI providers.
How it works
Step 1: Deploy tunnel client

# Install tunnel-client inside your network
# Run alongside the private MCP server
./tunnel-client --tunnel-id="abc123" --mcp-server="localhost:8080"

The client:
- Runs inside your network (wherever it can reach the private MCP server).
- Opens a persistent outbound HTTPS connection to the provider's control plane.
- Long-polls for queued work.
Step 2: Establish outbound connection

┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Enterprise Network (Private MCP Server)                  │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │ tunnel-client (gateway)                             │ │
│  │  • Binds to localhost:8080 (MCP server)             │ │
│  │  • Opens outbound HTTPS to provider's tunnel edge   │ │
│  │  • Long-polls for queued work                       │ │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│         ↓ Outbound HTTPS (no inbound firewall rule)       │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
         ↓
┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Provider's Tunnel Infrastructure (Cloudflare Edge)       │
│  • Receives outbound connection from tunnel-client        │
│  • Queues tool-call requests from the model               │
│  • Routes requests over the established connection        │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
The firewall sees only
outbound HTTPS
—no inbound rule required.
Step 3: Reverse request flow
When the model calls a tool:

1. Model → Provider's tunnel edge: "Call tool 'get_user_data'"
2. Provider queues request in the tunnel
3. tunnel-client long-polls → picks up queued request
4. tunnel-client → local MCP server (localhost:8080): JSON-RPC request
5. MCP server → tunnel-client: JSON-RPC response
6. tunnel-client → Provider's tunnel edge: Response
7. Provider → Model: Tool result
The MCP server
never initiates a connection
; it only responds to local requests.
Step 4: End-to-end encryption

tunnel-client → Cloudflare Edge: TLS (Cloudflare-managed)
Cloudflare Edge → Anthropic/OpenAI: Re-encrypted transport
You manage one secret: the gateway's tunnel credential.
Architecture patterns
Pattern 1: OpenAI Secure MCP Tunnel (GA)
OpenAI shipped
Secure MCP Tunnel

on May 28, 2026, as a GA enterprise feature:
-
tunnel-client

: Small binary that runs inside your network.
-
Outbound HTTPS

: Connects to OpenAI's control plane.
-
Long-poll

: Pulls queued work from OpenAI.
-
Supported
: ChatGPT Connectors, Codex, Responses API, AgentKit.

# Get tunnel_id from Platform Tunnels
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI()

# Create tunnel
tunnel = client.tunnels.create(
    name="private_mcp_server",
    mcp_server_url="localhost:8080"
)

# Use tunnel-backed private MCP server
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="codex",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Get user data"}],
    tools=[{"type": "mcp", "tunnel_id": tunnel.id}]
)
Pattern 2: Anthropic MCP Tunnels (Research Preview)
Anthropic introduced MCP tunnels for
Claude Managed Agents

in May 2026 (limited research preview):
-
Lightweight gateway

: Deploy inside your network.
-
Single outbound connection

: To Anthropic infrastructure via Cloudflare.
-
No inbound firewall rules

: No public endpoints.
-
End-to-end encryption
: TLS to edge, re-encrypted to Anthropic.
9to5mac

# Anthropic MCP tunnels (preview)
from anthropic import Anthropic

client = Anthropic()

# Register tunnel
tunnel = client.tunnels.register(
    name="internal_database",
    mcp_server_url="localhost:8080"
)

# Route agent through tunnel
agent = client.agents.create(
    name="backend_agent",
    tools=[{"type": "mcp", "tunnel_id": tunnel.id}]
)
Pattern 3: Cloudflare MCP Portals

Cloudflare MCP Portals centralize multiple MCP servers onto a single HTTP endpoint:
-
MCP server portal

: Aggregates multiple servers.
-
HTTP endpoint

: Single endpoint for all servers.
-
Cloudflare Access
: Gate access via identity provider.

# Cloudflare MCP Portal
from cloudflare import Cloudflare

client = Cloudflare()

# Create portal
portal = client.mcp_portals.create(
    name="internal_tools",
    servers=[
        {"name": "database", "url": "localhost:8080"},
        {"name": "ticketing", "url": "localhost:8081"}
    ]
)

# Connect tunnel to portal
tunnel = client.tunnels.create(
    name="internal_tools_tunnel",
    portal_id=portal.id
)
Benefits
No inbound firewall rule
: MCP server remains private; no inbound ports open.
Zero network exposure
: No public endpoint, no public DNS.
Security-friendly
: Outbound HTTPS is already allowed by most corporate networks.
Simplified deployment
: One secret (tunnel credential).
Enterprise-ready
: Works with multiple AI products simultaneously.
Trade-offs
Benefits:
- Aligns with
enterprise security requirements

(no inbound ports).
- Works with
any AI provider

(OpenAI, Anthropic, Cloudflare).
-
Open-source connector
: Uses Cloudflare's open-source tunnel connector.
Trade-offs:
Outbound-only reduces exposure but doesn't eliminate risk

: You still trust the MCP server, and the full tool-call payload transits the provider's control plane.
-
Governance is separate

: The tunnel handles transport; you need a governance layer (e.g., Stacklok) for access control.
-
Preview limitations
: Anthropic MCP Tunnels is a limited research preview (requires access request).
9to5mac
Best practices
Treat tunnel-client hosts like production infrastructure
: Patch the binary, rotate runtime keys, scope tunnels to the right workspace.
Wire in your identity provider before go-live
: Gate access via Okta, Microsoft Entra ID, or Google Workspace OIDC.
Scope each MCP server's permissions to minimum access
: Don't allow all users to access all tools.
Instrument request volume and audit logging
: Before go-live, not after an incident.
Don't treat the tunnel as your access control layer
: Use a governance layer (e.g., Stacklok) for tool-level policy.
Key takeaways
MCP tunnel =
outbound-only connectivity
that reverses the connection direction (client initiates, provider routes).
No inbound ports
: The MCP server never touches the public internet; firewall sees only outbound HTTPS.
Tunnel client/gateway
: Lightweight binary that long-polls for queued work.
End-to-end encryption
: TLS to edge + re-encrypted transport to provider.
Critical for
enterprise deployments
where security teams block inbound-port MCP deployments.
Best Practices
When working with mcp tunnel architecture (no inbound ports), following established best practices ensures reliability, maintainability, and optimal performance.
Start simple and iterate — begin with the minimum viable implementation before adding complexity.
Test with representative inputs — ensure your test cases cover edge cases, not just the happy path.
Monitor performance — track latency, token usage, and error rates in production environments.
Version your prompts and configurations — treat prompt text like source code with proper version control.
Document your design decisions — record why you chose specific approaches for future maintainers.
Plan for failure — implement graceful degradation when the AI component is unavailable or returns unexpected results.
Review outputs regularly — periodically audit what the system produces to catch quality drift over time.
Common Mistakes to Avoid
These pitfalls frequently trip up developers working with mcp tunnel architecture (no inbound ports). Knowing them in advance saves significant debugging time.
Assuming deterministic output — language models are probabilistic; the same input can produce different outputs across calls. Always validate the structure of responses.
Ignoring token limits — exceeding context window limits causes truncation or errors. Track token counts, especially in multi-turn conversations.
Over-engineering early — building complex infrastructure before validating basic functionality wastes effort. Get a working prototype first.
Hardcoding prompts in code — embed prompts in configuration files or databases so non-developers can iterate on them.
Skipping error handling — API calls fail due to rate limits, timeouts, or content policy violations. Always wrap calls in try/catch with appropriate retry logic.
Performance and Scalability
As your usage of mcp tunnel architecture (no inbound ports) scales, these performance factors become increasingly important.
Latency budget — typical API latency ranges from 1-30 seconds depending on model size and output length. Design your UX around streaming to provide early feedback.
Concurrency limits — each API tier has request-per-minute (RPM) and token-per-minute (TPM) limits. Implement request queuing for burst traffic.
Prompt caching — use the cache_control breakpoint on stable, repeated prompt prefixes to reduce costs by up to 90% and latency by 85%.
Batching — for non-interactive workloads, the Message Batches API processes up to 10,000 requests asynchronously at 50% cost reduction.
Model selection — use Claude Haiku for high-volume, low-complexity tasks and Claude Sonnet or Opus for complex reasoning where quality matters more than speed.
Best Practices
Follow these best practices when working with mcp tunnel architecture (no inbound ports) to maximize reliability and performance.
Start simple and iterate incrementally — validate your core approach before adding complexity.
Test with realistic inputs — use representative examples from your actual use case, not just toy examples.
Monitor in production — track latency, error rates, and output quality with structured logging and metrics.
Version control your configurations — treat prompts and settings like code with proper change tracking.
Handle errors gracefully — implement retry logic with exponential backoff for transient API errors.
Set appropriate timeouts — long-running requests should have explicit timeout limits to prevent hangs.
Review outputs regularly — periodically audit produced content to catch quality drift as models are updated.
Common Mistakes and How to Avoid Them
These pitfalls catch most developers off guard. Learn from them upfront.
Treating LLM output as deterministic — outputs vary between runs. Always validate structure and handle unexpected formats.
Not accounting for token limits — conversations grow over time. Implement context truncation strategies before hitting limits.
Over-engineering before validation — build the simplest thing that works, then optimize based on real measurements.
Ignoring rate limits — API throttling causes failures under load. Implement request queuing and rate-limit-aware retry logic.
Hardcoding prompts in source code — separate prompt text from code to enable rapid iteration without deployments.
Performance and Scalability Considerations
At scale, mcp tunnel architecture (no inbound ports) introduces specific performance challenges that require deliberate design decisions.
Latency: Claude Haiku responds in 1-5 seconds; Sonnet in 5-15 seconds; Opus in 15-30+ seconds. Choose the right model for your latency budget.
Throughput: Use async clients (AsyncAnthropic in Python, async/await in TypeScript) for concurrent requests rather than sequential blocking calls.
Cost optimization: Prompt caching reduces costs by 90% on repeated prefixes. The Message Batches API gives 50% discount for offline workloads.
Streaming: Use streaming responses to reduce perceived latency — users see output immediately rather than waiting for complete generation.
Connection pooling: Reuse HTTP connections with a persistent client instance rather than creating a new client per request.
Security and Reliability
Production deployments require attention to security boundaries and reliability engineering.
Never log API keys — rotate credentials immediately if they appear in logs, error messages, or version control history.
Validate and sanitize inputs — prompt injection attacks can exfiltrate data or override instructions if user inputs are embedded directly.
Implement circuit breakers — automatically stop sending requests when error rates exceed threshold to prevent cascading failures.
Audit all AI-generated actions — log every API call, input, and output with correlation IDs for debugging and compliance.
Summary

MCP tunnel architecture (no inbound ports) is a foundational aspect of building reliable Claude-powered applications. The patterns covered here — careful validation, incremental iteration, proper error handling, and performance-aware design — apply broadly across all Claude integrations. Master these fundamentals and you will be well-equipped to tackle more complex scenarios as your application evolves.
